speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 25, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Olga Drucker in Merrick, New York, USA. The language is English. And my name is Rhoda Fishler. Today is March 25, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Olga Drucker in Merrick, New York, USA. The language is English. And my name is Rhoda Fishler. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Olga Drucker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-L-G-A D-R-U-C-K-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olga Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-L-G-A. And I have a middle name, but I-- Maximiliane-- And L-E-V-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents changed the family name when I was about two years old, something like that, to Lenk, L-E-N-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they changed the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was for political reasons and also mainly for business reasons tied in together with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in December of 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-U-T-T-G-A-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a book publisher. Together with my uncle, his brother, they owned the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they publish any specific type  of books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they published children's books. And the whole business was founded by my grandfather, their father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was an old, established business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1872 or 1871. I forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Stuttgart also in October, October 11, 1880.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any special interests or hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than his own love for language-- he was a doctor of philology-- he collected stamps, and he liked to hike. He played music. He played the piano. Our house was always full of music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he alive today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in '70-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot. He was almost 93 years old, so I guess it would have been '73.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he die in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Martha, Martha Lenk. She was Martha Levy when she married my father. And her maiden name was Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: H-I-R-S-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Wiesbaden on September 24, 1891.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any interests outside of  the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, she loved to read. She sewed. Also, she was musical. She used to sing in a chorus. She-- I think she just liked the arts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she alive today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1967 of cancer in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a brother. He's my only brother, my only sibling. He's older than I am. His name is Herbert Lenk. In Germany, his name was Hans, H-A-N-S, Hans Herbert. And when he became an American, he switched it around to Herbert Hans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live today?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's now in Florida. He and his wife lived in, in Cincinnati for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your pre-war address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Caesar Fleischlenstrasse funf-- five. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: As close to it as you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-A-E-S-A-R, Caesar. Fleischlen is F-L-E-I-S-C-H-L-E-N, and strasse means street. S-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your home looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was-- well, this was a house that my father had built, and my uncle had a similar house right next door. And they were Bauhaus-style houses. They were square with flat roofs, stucco, three floors with verandas and big windows and a nice garden for me to play in. I also could play on, on the roof because it was flat with a fence around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other members of your household living in this home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides my parents and my brother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not of the family. But we had a-- we had a cook, and we had a, a maid, the woman that pretty much brought me up, actually. And her name was Frieda. I'm not sure what her last name was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollections of what Jewish life was like as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jewish life, I'm not sure. It was just life for me, because I wasn't really taught anything about being Jewish. I, I, other-- I just knew that we were Jewish. When-- from what time on I knew that I, I really don't remember. But we didn't have much of a Jewish life at all. I remember one time going to a synagogue with my uncle. Perhaps it was a holiday, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your family as a whole did not attend synagogue regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. And as a matter of fact, we did have, as did quite a few other German Jewish families, I think we had a Christmas tree, ostensibly for the maid, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you exchange Christmas gifts--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we enjoyed it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any other languages, or did your parents communicate in any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. I don't know. I think most of their friends and acquaintances-- well, friends, let's say, must have been Jewish, I think. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about yourself? Did you have Jewish friends, non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, both.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both from school, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, tell me about school. When did you start going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started going to school quite late actually because I was very ill when I was a little girl. I had a middle ear infection. And my mother-- my mother didn't want me to-- she thought I was delicate, which I'm far from, never was. But anyway, she held me back for a year so that by the time I did get to school, I was able to read and write already. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: --seven years old. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started school at age seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a public or a private school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a private girls' school. And, and I had a teacher who-- in Germany I don't know if it's still the same. The teachers move up with their class. They don't get a new teacher every time they move up. So Fraulein [NON-ENGLISH] was my teacher, and I loved her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in this class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have to count them. I have a picture of them. But I'd say about between 15 and 20 perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what you were learning at school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we certainly learned to write. You know, we had composition. We had arithmetic, of course. And we must have had some history and geography. I know we had music. We  all learned the recorder, played a recorder. We had gymnastics. And that was it. Then I think there was some-- I'm going to say something I'm not sure about, that I know that the children had to have religious instruction, and I'm not sure what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't recall--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't recall whether the Jewish children were taken out of the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably were, so-- but I don't remember getting any Jewish instruction until later on when I had to. But that comes later in the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when you had your first anti-Semitic experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, there wasn't much of that. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me think. I have to think about that. There was one. This was after Kristallnacht already. One of my friends, a non-Jewish friend, met me coming towards her in the street. And when-- and I had already-- I wasn't going to that school anymore. I was by then going to a Jewish school. And she crossed the street without waving to me or without recognizing me, and I was devastated. And I guess you could call that an anti-Semitic incident because that's when my mother explained to me that I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, except that it was not good. It wasn't something good, because everybody was against us, you know. And we were supposed to be so, such terrible people, and I never had anything very positive about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, a little while ago, you mentioned Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened on Kristallnacht and when it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that I remember vividly. Well, November 9, the night of November 9 and 10 in 1938. And I remember waking up because there were-- I heard some people coming into the house in the middle of the night--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and coming up the steps. I was almost 11. I was still 10 years old. And I was very scared. I didn't know who was in this house and why they were making such a racket and banging doors. And, I, I heard my mother crying at one point. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then everything got quiet, and she came into my room. And I, I didn't-- I couldn't face it, I guess. Denial is sometimes my middle name. And so I pretended to be asleep, and she went out again. But that was the night that they came for my father, as they came for all males-- we learned later-- between 16 and 60 or 80. I don't know what the cut-off date was. And, and then my mother got on the phone and tried to find out where they took him. And she called the police station, and they had taken, taken him there. My uncle next door was there, too. And from there, they had taken him to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how she found out where he was taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The police informed her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did Dachau mean anything to your mother at that time? Did she know what it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what it meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Well, perhaps she did know. I, I-- at that time, I knew it was a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know what that meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever discuss this with your peers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we never really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my old school, no. It was a-- I guess a few days went by, and then the-- and then I was sent to a Jewish school. These Jewish schools were open, schools were opened up immediately so that the children could go on going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who opened these schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish community was well organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What can you tell me about this Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I remember-- and it was only a few months that I was going there anyway. What I remember, it was November, so Hanukkah was coming up, and I learned about Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you known anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew. And when I came home to tell my mother about it-- because at that point, everybody was gone. My, my maid, Frieda, was no longer with us because she wasn't-- I'm getting to Hanukkah-- because she, she wasn't allowed by, by the Nuremberg laws to work for us anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the rationale for that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rationale? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, their rationale.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, any young, so-called Aryan woman under a certain age-- let's make it 35. I'm not, I'm not positive about that-- couldn't work for Jewish people anymore probably-- I don't know, the Jewish people were going to do something dreadful to them. Anyway. And, and my brother was already in England in school at that time, and now my father was gone. So it was just my mother and I. And I came home and told her about Hanukkah. And she took me by the hand, and we went up to the third floor, where there was like a storage room, and she found this, this Hanukkah menorah. And she must have-- I mean, she must have known that it was there all along. But she brought that out, and we actually had a Hanukkah. I had a sheet with the songs on it and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lit the candles--

speaker: interviewee
text: My first Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for Hanukkah--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lit the candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father-- or did your father return home?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did return after, I think, it was seven or eight weeks. Well, he came home on my birthday, which is on the 28th of December. And-- but he wasn't allowed to talk about Dachau, and he never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did his physical appearance--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --change in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very thin and very old-looking, and he had frostbite on his toes. And it was bad, but it was good to have him back home again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did his demeanor change in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I, I think-- he was never the most cheerful of men, I mean, but he would crack a joke every now and then. But after that, it was like everything was dead serious, you know. Everything was so serious. And of course, I don't know if you want me to talk about this. At this point, when my father was already in Dachau, my mother made the decision to have my name placed on the Kindertransport list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain the Kindertransport.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was from that night. Everything stems back to that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kristallnacht, yes. Excuse me. [DRINKS] And she heard about this-- this, this was an organized plan to rescue as many children-- rescue-- to get children out of Germany. At that point, everybody knew that it was not good to stay there anymore. So the thing was to get the children out as fast as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was organized by the Jewish community throughout Germany and Austria, Czechoslovakia and Poland as well, a few in Poland. And also not only Jewish, but Christian. A lot of Quakers were involved with it. And we were going to be taken to England. And this was a, a tremendous undertaking. In England, they had to pass an act of Parliament to get the funding to-- and, and to get people to take us in. It was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were private families that would take us. There were institutions that would take us. Some of them were very Orthodox Jewish organizations. Others were-- it was the whole spectrum. It didn't have to be Jewish at all.  As a matter of fact, I-- after my first-- I'm jumping the gun-- but after my first experience with a Jewish family, I, I was living with Christian people all along-- the rest of the time that I was in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's backtrack a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know. I'm jumping ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. You said that your mother began to make arrangements for you to go on the Kindertransport. What did these arrangements consist of? What did she have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. She, she had to just put my name on a list. Whether she paid any money for this, I don't know. She-- I know that that wasn't the requirement. You didn't have to pay for it. But since we were well off, I, I wouldn't be surprised if she did contribute to it. And then, she-- of course, she had to prepare me. You know, I was a little girl, and she had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: --get me used to it. 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she prepare you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she got a teacher in to teach me some English, and that's number one, I guess. And I learned to say, the dog is under the table. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. Also, there were things to pack, what I could take.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too much, actually. Most of the stuff I never saw anymore. But clothing, and she actually packed up what amounts to a trousseau, I think, you know, bed sheets and that sort of thing, which, to this day, I know I don't have them anymore, but I did have for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then this committee, the Refugee Committee, it was called, sent us the name of the family that I was to be with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had the name and address.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that family sent us some letters and a picture of themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you established some kind of communication before you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this whole process take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I didn't actually go till March, so from November to March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that period, were you still going to this Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still going to the Jewish school. Then I learned about Purim and Haman. And I said Haman-- I told my mother that Haman's just like Hitler. And she went pale because you're not allowed to say things, not even in your own house, you know. The saying used to be   that the walls had ears, and they might well have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening to your schoolmates at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They just went on going to school because they weren't Jewish. And the Jewish schoolmates, I don't even remember them anymore. I think I must have blocked a lot of stuff, and also it was such a short time from November to March that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on that March day when you left on the Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents and I went in the business car to the railroad station. Somebody else drove us because neither of my parents drove.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was still in business at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, no. Not really, no. The business had been taken from him a while. Since 1936 he had been forced to sell the business. This is now 1930-- early 1939. So he was really in a-- but we still had a car available. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some things don't make any sense as I tell them, but this is how I remember it. Anyway, we went to the station, and my mother was allowed to come on the train with me as far as Wiesbaden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about, I'd say, about an hour on the train. It was early morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the other children on the  train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my cousin was with me, my cousin Hilda, who's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who lived next door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a pretty close relationship with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's three years older, so she was 14 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she sort of taking care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I don't think so. I don't know. I seem to remember that I was pretty alone. Well, my mother was still with me for a little while anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [LAUGHS] Isn't it funny the things you remember? I had on a blue woolen dress. And it was wool, and I-- to this day, I'm allergic to wool. But who knew from allergic in those days, right? So I wore this blue dress, and I had underpants to match. And I remember an incident on that train where I had to go to the bathroom. And when I came out of it, everybody was pointing at me and laughing. And I looked down, and I had my dress caught in my underpants. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's tragic at that age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember it, like, 60 years late-- no, not-- 50 years later. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the train itself like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have the whole train. It was not a special train. It was a regular passenger train, and we might have had one or two cars. I can't say for sure, but there might have been about 100 kids on that train. And we were all very excited, of course. And the kids were like from very, very young, three, four years old, to-- the cut-off age was 17. So it was all a mixture of kids. And of course, we all had to-- wanted to be by the window, you know. And I know I, I was able to wave good-bye to my father in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents present this trip to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said they would come to England in about six weeks after, and I believed them. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your mother was on the train with you to Wiesbaden. Were there other adults on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think not too many, except for the German officers that were on the train with us, you know, they were the SS people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What role did they play?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. To keep us in line or to make sure that we were going where we were going, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I have-- I don't remember anything bad happening on my particular train. But I've heard other children who had come out on other Kindertransports, where these SS people were really nasty and opened up their suitcases and just threw everything around and really mean. But I honestly don't remember anything like that happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived at Wiesbaden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then my-- this was the town that my mother was born in and where my grandmother still lived, and she came to the train station to say good-bye to me. Again, I don't think I understood the, the gravity of it. I don't think I understood that perhaps this would be the last time we would see each other. And indeed, it was the last time I would see my grandmother because later she was killed in Theresienstadt. But she brought me a little present.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It, it isn't the original, but it was exactly like that. Where should I hold it, like that? It was-- it's-- actually, there were two books. One was this color, and the other one was red. And this one's from German to English, and the other one was from English to German. And this is how I got by the first few weeks that I was in England, because just to be able to say that the dog's under the table doesn't help you very much. And so if I wanted to communicate, I used the dictionaries and the other way around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you left Wiesbaden?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? Well, my mother got off in Wiesbaden, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I was on my own on the train. And we got to-- I guess it must have taken pretty much a full day when we finally arrived in the Hook of Holland in Holland, which is where we were to get on the boat to go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we got to the border of Germany and Holland, the German officials got off, and the Dutch officials came on. And I guess we had to-- there must have been some other adults with us because they carried our papers, our passports. We all had numbers hanging around our necks and little cardboard things. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, the Dutch officials were much more human, even human-looking, you know. Are you OK? I'm fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: What else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the food that you ate on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if we ate. We must have had something on the train. I don't remember. I know that we were greeted with stuff in Holland. There were some women, Dutch women, there who were ready for us with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was hot chocolate and cookies and stuff. It wasn't-- it wasn't a full meal. I don't think it was even sandwiches. But it was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were taken onto the ferry boats. And this was already evening. It was an overnight [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] journey. And I was-- I had lost my cousin by then. We didn't see each other anymore. She must have been on that boat, too, but we weren't kept together. And I was put on a, on a, in a room with a-- a cabin with an older girl, and she cried all the way over. And so that's when I started crying, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
